title: Cardiology Essentials — USMLE Step 1
description: Heart anatomy, conduction system, common arrhythmias, heart failure, valvular disease, and key drugs. High-yield USMLE Step 1 content.
subject: medicine

cards.front: What is the normal PR interval?
cards.back: 0.12–0.20 seconds (3–5 small squares on ECG)

cards.front: What is the normal QRS duration?
cards.back: < 0.12 seconds (< 3 small squares)

cards.front: Atrial fibrillation ECG findings
cards.back: Irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline

cards.front: First-line treatment for stable atrial fibrillation with RVR
cards.back: Rate control: β-blocker (metoprolol) or calcium channel blocker (diltiazem)

cards.front: What causes a wide QRS complex?
cards.back: Bundle branch block, ventricular ectopy, pre-excitation (WPW), hyperkalemia, sodium channel blocker toxicity

cards.front: Classic ECG findings in STEMI of the LAD territory
cards.back: ST elevation in V1–V4 (anterior leads)

cards.front: Classic ECG findings in right coronary artery (inferior) STEMI
cards.back: ST elevation in II, III, aVF

cards.front: What is Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome?
cards.back: Accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent) bypasses AV node → delta wave, short PR interval, widened QRS, risk of sudden death

cards.front: What drug is contraindicated in WPW with AF?
cards.back: AV nodal blockers (digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem) — may accelerate conduction down accessory pathway

cards.front: Mechanism of action of digoxin
cards.back: Inhibits Na+/K+ ATPase → increases intracellular Na+ → increases intracellular Ca2+ via Na+/Ca2+ exchanger → positive inotropy

cards.front: Signs of digoxin toxicity
cards.back: Nausea, vomiting, visual disturbances (yellow-green halos), arrhythmias (AV block, ventricular tachycardia)

cards.front: What is cardiac tamponade?
cards.back: Fluid in pericardial sac compresses heart → decreased cardiac output. Beck's triad: hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds

cards.front: What is pulsus paradoxus?
cards.back: Drop in systolic BP > 10 mmHg during inspiration; classic in cardiac tamponade and severe asthma

cards.front: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF): key drugs that reduce mortality
cards.back: ACE inhibitor (or ARB/ARNI), β-blocker, aldosterone antagonist (spironolactone/eplerenone), SGLT2 inhibitor

cards.front: What is the Frank-Starling law?
cards.back: Stroke volume increases with increased preload (ventricular end-diastolic volume) — up to a physiologic limit

cards.front: Systolic vs diastolic heart failure
cards.back: Systolic (HFrEF): reduced EF < 40%, dilated ventricle. Diastolic (HFpEF): preserved EF ≥ 50%, stiff ventricle

cards.front: Mitral stenosis: cause and murmur
cards.back: Most commonly rheumatic heart disease. Opening snap + low-pitched rumbling mid-diastolic murmur at apex; louder in left lateral decubitus

cards.front: Aortic stenosis: murmur characteristics
cards.back: Harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border; radiates to carotids. Classic triad: angina, syncope, heart failure

cards.front: Mitral regurgitation: murmur
cards.back: Holosystolic (pansystolic) murmur at apex, radiating to axilla

cards.front: Aortic regurgitation: murmur and sign
cards.back: High-pitched blowing early diastolic murmur at left sternal border. Austin Flint murmur. Wide pulse pressure

cards.front: What is the most common cause of aortic stenosis in the US?
cards.back: Degenerative calcification (senile/age-related), especially in patients > 70 years

cards.front: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM): murmur changes
cards.back: Systolic ejection murmur that INCREASES with Valsalva (decreased preload) and DECREASES with squatting

cards.front: What is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes?
cards.back: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HOCM)

cards.front: Endocarditis prophylaxis: indications
cards.back: High-risk cardiac conditions (prosthetic valves, previous IE, certain congenital defects) + dental procedures causing gingival bleeding

cards.front: Most common organism in subacute bacterial endocarditis
cards.back: Viridans streptococci (after dental procedures)

cards.front: Most common organism in acute bacterial endocarditis (IV drug users)
cards.back: Staphylococcus aureus (tricuspid valve)

cards.front: Janeway lesions vs Osler nodes in endocarditis
cards.back: Janeway = flat, painless erythematous macules on palms/soles (emboli). Osler nodes = tender nodules on finger/toe pads (immune complex)

cards.front: First-line treatment for hypertensive emergency
cards.back: IV antihypertensives: labetalol, nicardipine, or sodium nitroprusside. Goal: reduce MAP by ≤ 25% in first hour

cards.front: What is orthostatic hypotension?
cards.back: Drop in systolic BP ≥ 20 mmHg or diastolic BP ≥ 10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing

cards.front: Mechanism of action of ACE inhibitors
cards.back: Block conversion of angiotensin I → angiotensin II; also prevent bradykinin degradation (causes cough/angioedema)

cards.front: Mechanism of action of β-blockers
cards.back: Block β1 (and β2) adrenergic receptors → decreased heart rate, contractility, and renin release

cards.front: What is Torsades de Pointes?
cards.back: Polymorphic VT with QTc > 500ms. Causes: hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, drugs (haloperidol, amiodarone, macrolides). Treatment: IV magnesium

cards.front: Long QT causes (mnemonic)
cards.back: HALT: Hypokalemia, Antiarrhythmics (class IA/III), Long QT syndrome, Thyroid (hypothyroidism)

cards.front: Amiodarone: major adverse effects
cards.back: Pulmonary toxicity, hepatotoxicity, thyroid dysfunction (hypo/hyper), corneal microdeposits, blue-grey skin discoloration, peripheral neuropathy

cards.front: What is the maximum dose of adenosine?
cards.back: 6 mg IV push first, then 12 mg (can repeat 12 mg once). Used for SVT; very short half-life (< 10 seconds)

cards.front: What is the difference between preload and afterload?
cards.back: Preload = ventricular end-diastolic pressure/volume (↑ with fluid overload). Afterload = resistance against which ventricle ejects (↑ with systemic hypertension, aortic stenosis)

cards.front: Coronary artery that supplies the SA node (in most people)
cards.back: Right coronary artery (RCA) in ~60% of people

cards.front: What vessel does the left anterior descending (LAD) artery supply?
cards.back: Anterior 2/3 of interventricular septum, anterior wall of left ventricle, apex

cards.front: What is a right heart (Swan-Ganz) catheter used to measure?
cards.back: Pulmonary artery pressure, PCWP (reflects left atrial pressure/preload), cardiac output via thermodilution

cards.front: What is the Fick principle?
cards.back: Cardiac output = O2 consumption / (arterial O2 content − venous O2 content)

cards.front: Cyanotic congenital heart disease — right-to-left shunts (5 Ts)
cards.back: Tetralogy of Fallot, Transposition of great arteries, Truncus arteriosus, Tricuspid atresia, Total anomalous pulmonary venous return

cards.front: Tetralogy of Fallot — 4 components
cards.back: Pulmonary stenosis, Right ventricular hypertrophy, Overriding aorta, VSD (PROVe)

cards.front: Most common congenital heart defect overall
cards.back: Ventricular septal defect (VSD)

cards.front: What murmur is heard in VSD?
cards.back: Holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border; louder with small VSDs (maladie de Roger)

cards.front: Patent ductus arteriosus: murmur and treatment
cards.back: Continuous machine-like murmur. Treatment: indomethacin (NSAID closes ductus). Alprostadil (PGE1) keeps it open

cards.front: What is coarctation of the aorta?
cards.back: Narrowing of aorta, classically near ligamentum arteriosum. Features: upper extremity hypertension, rib notching (collaterals), lower extremity weakness

cards.front: Eisenmenger syndrome
cards.back: Left-to-right shunt that reverses to right-to-left due to pulmonary hypertension. Results in cyanosis; contraindication to surgical repair

cards.front: Brugada syndrome: ECG pattern and risk
cards.back: Right bundle branch block + ST elevation in V1–V3. Risk of sudden cardiac death; treated with ICD

cards.front: What is Long QT syndrome type 1 (LQT1) triggered by?
cards.back: Exercise (especially swimming)

cards.front: What is the first-line treatment for ventricular fibrillation?
cards.back: Immediate defibrillation (unsynchronized cardioversion) + CPR + epinephrine
